enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source web front end for managing CalDAV tasks and calendars across multiple accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Manage My Damn Life (MMDL) is a free, open-source, self-hosted front end for CalDAV tasks and calendars. Rather than replacing your calendar server, it acts as a unified interface that connects to existing CalDAV backends such as Nextcloud, Baikal, and Radicale, plus Google Calendar over OAuth.

The project is built with Next.js and distributed on GitHub under the intri-in organization, with Docker deployment support for people who want to run it on their own infrastructure alongside their existing calendar server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MMDL is aimed at self-hosters who already run a CalDAV server and want a more capable task-and-calendar interface than their server's default web UI, without handing calendar data to a third-party SaaS product.

features.title: Multi-account CalDAV support
features.description: Connect several CalDAV accounts and manage them from one interface, using the tsdav library for protocol access.

features.title: Multiple task and calendar views
features.description: Switch between list, calendar, and Gantt-chart views of the same underlying tasks and events.

features.title: Subtasks and rich task fields
features.description: Tasks support due dates, status, description, and recurrence rules.

features.title: Nextcloud, Baikal, and Radicale compatibility
features.description: Tested against these open-source CalDAV servers, with broader server support expected via the tsdav library.

features.title: Google Calendar OAuth
features.description: Connect a Google Calendar account alongside self-hosted CalDAV accounts.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Ships with a sample docker-compose file for self-hosting.

pros: Free and open source, no vendor lock-in
Works with popular self-hosted CalDAV servers you may already run
Multiple views (list, calendar, Gantt) for the same task data
Supports multiple accounts and both CalDAV and Google Calendar
cons: Requires you to already run (or set up) a separate CalDAV server such as Nextcloud, Baikal, or Radicale
Small community project — support relies on the GitHub repository rather than dedicated documentation or a company
Self-hosting and Docker familiarity required to deploy it

faqs.question: Do I need a CalDAV server to use Manage My Damn Life?
faqs.answer: Yes. MMDL is a front end only — it connects to an existing CalDAV server such as Nextcloud, Baikal, or Radicale (or to Google Calendar via OAuth).

faqs.question: Is Manage My Damn Life free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source, distributed on GitHub.

faqs.question: How do I deploy it?
faqs.answer: The project provides a sample docker-compose configuration for self-hosted deployment.

use_cases.title: Unified task/calendar dashboard
use_cases.description: Combine tasks and events from several CalDAV accounts and Google Calendar in one interface.

use_cases.title: Project-style task tracking
use_cases.description: Use the Gantt view to plan tasks with due dates and recurrence on top of a CalDAV backend.
use_cases.audience: Individuals who want lightweight project tracking without a SaaS tool

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Manage My Damn Life is a free, open-source, self-hosted web front end for managing CalDAV tasks and calendars from Nextcloud, Baikal, or Radicale.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, free CalDAV and CardDAV server for self-hosting calendars, to-do lists, and contacts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Radicale is a small but capable CalDAV (calendars, to-do lists, journal entries) and CardDAV (contacts) server, released under the GPLv3 license and maintained by the Kozea community. It's written in Python 3.9+ and can run as a standalone HTTP server or as a WSGI application behind another web server.

It stores data directly on the filesystem in a simple folder structure, avoiding a database dependency, and works out of the box with minimal configuration. Radicale supports TLS, pluggable authentication and access-control backends, and runs on Linux, BSD, macOS, and Windows, as well as via Docker (amd64 and arm64).

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Radicale suits individuals and small teams who want to self-host calendar and contact sync (compatible with standard CalDAV/CardDAV clients like Apple Calendar/Contacts, Thunderbird, and DAVx5) without running a heavier groupware suite.

features.title: CalDAV support
features.description: Sync events, to-dos, and journal entries to any standard CalDAV client.

features.title: CardDAV support
features.description: Sync contacts/address books to any standard CardDAV client.

features.title: Filesystem storage
features.description: Data is stored as plain files, simplifying backup and inspection.

features.title: Pluggable authentication and rights
features.description: Extensible auth, rights-management, storage, and web-interface plugin system.

features.title: TLS support
features.description: Secure client connections out of the box.

features.title: Cross-platform
features.description: Runs on Linux, BSD, macOS, Windows, and via Docker.

pros: Genuinely lightweight — minimal dependencies and simple filesystem-based storage
No complicated setup required to get a working CalDAV/CardDAV server
Free and open source under GPLv3
Works with any standard CalDAV/CardDAV client
cons: No built-in web UI comparable to full groupware suites — it's a protocol server, not a polished app
Scaling and multi-user administration is more manual than hosted alternatives
Community-maintained project, not a commercial product with SLAs

faqs.question: Is Radicale free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Radicale is free, open-source software licensed under GPLv3.

faqs.question: What protocols does Radicale support?
faqs.answer: CalDAV for calendars and to-do lists, and CardDAV for contacts.

faqs.question: Does Radicale need a database?
faqs.answer: No, it stores data directly on the filesystem.

faqs.question: Can I use Radicale with Apple Calendar or Thunderbird?
faqs.answer: Yes, any standard CalDAV/CardDAV client can connect to a Radicale server.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted calendar sync
use_cases.description: Sync calendars and to-do lists across devices without a third-party cloud provider.

use_cases.title: Backend for CalDAV front ends
use_cases.description: Serve as the storage backend for other apps and front ends that speak CalDAV/CardDAV.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosters building a personal productivity stack

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Radicale is a free, open-source CalDAV and CardDAV server for self-hosting calendars, to-do lists, and contacts with minimal setup.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted realtime WebSocket server with delivery guarantees, built to offload real-time features from web app backends.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AnyCable is a standalone realtime server, written in Go, that handles persistent WebSocket connections on behalf of a web application backend. It adds delivery guarantees (recovering messages missed during a disconnect), presence tracking, message ordering, and connection persistence across backend deployments.

It's built and maintained by Evil Martians and released under the MIT license as open-source software, with an optional paid Pro tier and a managed SaaS offering for teams that don't want to operate it themselves. AnyCable integrates with Rails/Hotwire and Turbo Streams natively, and can be used from Laravel, Node.js, Python, or any backend that can call its HTTP API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: AnyCable is aimed at engineering teams — particularly Rails/Hotwire shops, but also polyglot teams — who need reliable real-time features (chat, live dashboards, notifications, presence) at a scale where a request/response backend's built-in WebSocket handling (e.g. Action Cable) becomes a bottleneck.

features.title: Delivery guarantees
features.description: Automatically recovers messages missed during connectivity interruptions.

features.title: Connection persistence
features.description: Keeps WebSocket connections alive across backend application deployments.

features.title: High connection capacity
features.description: Designed to support 10,000+ concurrent connections per server instance.

features.title: Presence tracking
features.description: Track which users/clients are currently connected to a channel.

features.title: Framework integrations
features.description: Native support for Rails/Hotwire/Turbo Streams, plus HTTP API access from Laravel, Node.js, Python, and other backends.

features.title: On-premise deployment
features.description: Self-hosted option keeps connection data on your own infrastructure.

pros: Open-source core is free and self-hostable under MIT license
Written in Go for low memory overhead on long-lived connections
Built-in delivery guarantees and reconnection handling reduce custom real-time code
Strong Rails/Hotwire integration from a well-known Ruby consultancy (Evil Martians)
cons: Advanced features (adaptive scaling, binary compression, dedicated support) are gated behind the paid Pro tier
Adds an extra service to operate and monitor alongside your main backend
Best-documented for Rails/Hotwire; other backend integrations rely on a generic HTTP API

faqs.question: Is AnyCable free to self-host?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source core is free and MIT-licensed. A paid Pro tier (listed at $1,490/year) adds features like adaptive scaling and dedicated support.

faqs.question: What frameworks does AnyCable work with?
faqs.answer: It has native integration with Rails/Hotwire/Turbo Streams and can be used from Laravel, Node.js, Python, or any backend via its HTTP API.

faqs.question: Who builds AnyCable?
faqs.answer: AnyCable is developed by Evil Martians and has been used in production since 2017.

use_cases.title: Scaling WebSocket connections
use_cases.description: Offload persistent connection handling from a Rails/Hotwire or other backend to a dedicated Go server.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams outgrowing built-in WebSocket handling

use_cases.title: Real-time notifications and dashboards
use_cases.description: Push live updates (dashboards, tracking, chat) to clients with delivery guarantees.

features: Core delivery guarantees and connection handling

enrichment.seo_meta_description: AnyCable is a self-hosted, MIT-licensed realtime WebSocket server with delivery guarantees, built by Evil Martians for Rails, Laravel, and Node.js apps.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted real-time messaging server for broadcasting updates over WebSocket and other transports.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Centrifugo is a self-hosted, MIT-licensed real-time messaging server written in Go. It manages persistent client connections and broadcasts messages over WebSocket, HTTP streaming, SSE, WebTransport, or gRPC, acting as a self-hosted alternative to hosted services like Pusher, Ably, or PubNub.

It supports PUB/SUB messaging, channel history, online presence, and clustering via Redis, PostgreSQL, or NATS as a broker, making it suitable for broadcasting to large numbers of concurrently connected clients. A commercial Centrifugo PRO edition adds features like analytics, real-time tracing, push notification APIs, and SSO, licensed separately from the open-source core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Centrifugo is aimed at backend engineering teams who need to add real-time features — chat, live comments, multiplayer state sync, live dashboards, or AI token streaming — to an existing application without depending on a hosted real-time SaaS provider.

features.title: Multiple transport protocols
features.description: WebSocket, HTTP-streaming, Server-Sent Events, WebTransport, and gRPC.

features.title: PUB/SUB and state sync
features.description: Broadcast messages and synchronize state to subscribed clients.

features.title: Channel history and presence
features.description: Track channel history and which clients are currently online.

features.title: Clustering via Redis/PostgreSQL/NATS
features.description: Scale across multiple nodes using a supported broker/engine.

features.title: Client SDKs
features.description: Official SDKs for web and mobile clients, with a language-agnostic backend publishing API.

features.title: Centrifugo PRO add-ons
features.description: Commercial edition adds analytics, tracing, push notification APIs, and SSO integrations.

pros: Open-source core is free, MIT-licensed, and self-hostable
Language-agnostic — any backend can publish messages via its API
Built for horizontal scaling to large numbers of concurrent connections
Supports several transport protocols beyond plain WebSocket
cons: Advanced operational features (analytics, tracing, SSO) require the paid PRO edition with custom, company-size-based pricing
Requires running and operating a separate broker (Redis/PostgreSQL/NATS) for multi-node clustering
Adds infrastructure to manage compared to a fully hosted real-time API

faqs.question: Is Centrifugo free?
faqs.answer: The core Centrifugo server is free and open source under the MIT license. Centrifugo PRO is a separately licensed commercial edition with custom pricing based on company size.

faqs.question: What transports does Centrifugo support?
faqs.answer: WebSocket, HTTP-streaming, Server-Sent Events, WebTransport, and gRPC.

faqs.question: Can Centrifugo scale across multiple servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, using Redis, PostgreSQL, or NATS as a broker for clustering.

use_cases.title: Live chat and comments
use_cases.description: Broadcast chat messages or comment updates to connected clients in real time.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams building chat or live-comment features

use_cases.title: Live dashboards and data feeds
use_cases.description: Stream analytics, financial, or IoT sensor data to connected clients.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Centrifugo is an open-source, self-hosted real-time messaging server for broadcasting over WebSocket, SSE, and gRPC, with an optional PRO edition.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source peer-to-peer chat app that runs serverless in the browser with no accounts and no message storage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chitchatter is a free, open-source, GPLv2-licensed chat application that connects users directly, browser-to-browser, using WebRTC — there's no chat backend storing messages. Rooms are ephemeral: when a peer leaves, messages held only in that peer's memory are gone.

It requires only static hosting (the project itself is served from GitHub Pages) plus public WebTorrent trackers and TURN relay servers to help establish peer connections; no application server or database is needed. Because it's open source, it can also be self-hosted as a static site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Chitchatter suits people who want a quick, no-signup, privacy-oriented chat room for a conversation, file transfer, or screen share, without relying on a server that could log or retain messages.

features.title: Serverless peer-to-peer chat
features.description: Messages travel directly between browsers over WebRTC with no backend server.

features.title: Public and private rooms
features.description: Create shareable public rooms or private, name-gated rooms.

features.title: Video, audio, and screen sharing
features.description: Supports video/audio chat and screen sharing between peers in a room.

features.title: Encrypted file transfer
features.description: Files are encrypted before sending and decrypted by the receiver, with no practical size limit.

features.title: Markdown messages
features.description: Message text supports Markdown formatting and code syntax highlighting.

features.title: Ephemeral by design
features.description: Messages are never persisted to disk and disappear from memory when a peer leaves.

pros: No account, sign-up, or server-side message storage required
Free and open source under GPLv2, can be self-hosted as a static site
Supports video, audio, screen share, and encrypted file transfer, not just text
Ephemeral messaging by design reduces data-retention risk
cons: Relies on public WebTorrent trackers and TURN servers for peer discovery/relay, which are outside your control
No message history or persistence — anything not backfilled from a connected peer is lost
As a browser-based P2P tool, reliability can vary with network conditions (NAT/firewall traversal)

faqs.question: Does Chitchatter store my messages?
faqs.answer: No. Messages exist only in each connected browser's memory and are cleared when you leave a room.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use Chitchatter?
faqs.answer: No, there's no sign-up — you join a room by name and start chatting.

faqs.question: Is Chitchatter self-hostable?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under GPLv2, so it can be deployed as a static site rather than using the hosted chitchatter.im instance.

use_cases.title: Quick private conversation
use_cases.description: Spin up a named room for a one-off private chat without creating an account.

use_cases.title: Ad hoc file or screen sharing
use_cases.description: Share files or a screen directly between browsers without uploading to a server.
use_cases.audience: People needing a quick, serverless P2P session

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chitchatter is a free, open-source, serverless peer-to-peer chat app with video, screen sharing, and no message storage or sign-up.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, open-source Matrix homeserver in Rust for self-hosting your own federated chat server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Conduit is a Matrix homeserver implementation written in Rust, released under the Apache-2.0 license by developer Timo Koesters. It's designed to be a single binary with an embedded RocksDB database, aiming for low system requirements and a quick setup compared to larger homeservers like Synapse.

Because it implements the Matrix protocol, a self-hosted Conduit server can federate with the wider Matrix network and use bridges to connect to platforms like Discord. The project describes itself as still in beta — most Matrix rooms and features work, but some smaller features may be missing or buggy. It received German government (BMBF/Prototype Fund) funding for part of its development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Conduit is aimed at self-hosters who want a lightweight, low-maintenance Matrix homeserver — for example running a small personal or community Matrix server — without the resource overhead of larger homeserver implementations.

features.title: Single-binary deployment
features.description: Ships as one binary with an embedded RocksDB database, simplifying setup.

features.title: Matrix protocol / federation
features.description: Federates with the wider Matrix network like other Matrix homeservers.

features.title: Low system requirements
features.description: Built to run on modest hardware compared to larger Matrix homeserver implementations.

features.title: Bridge compatibility
features.description: Can be paired with Matrix bridges to connect to platforms like Discord.

pros: Free, open source (Apache-2.0), and self-hostable
Simple single-binary setup with no external database to manage
Lower resource footprint than larger Matrix homeserver implementations
Interoperable with the broader federated Matrix network
cons: Officially labeled beta — some smaller Matrix features may be missing or unreliable
Smaller contributor base than the reference Synapse homeserver
Community forks (like Continuwuity) have diverged with additional features and more active recent development

faqs.question: Is Conduit free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Conduit is free, open-source software (Apache-2.0 license); the project also accepts donations.

faqs.question: What is Conduit built with?
faqs.answer: It's written in Rust and uses an embedded RocksDB database, packaged as a single binary.

faqs.question: Is Conduit production-ready?
faqs.answer: The project describes itself as beta — functional for most Matrix use, but potentially missing smaller features.

use_cases.title: Personal or small-community Matrix server
use_cases.description: Self-host a lightweight homeserver to chat on the Matrix network under your own domain.
use_cases.audience: Individuals and small communities self-hosting chat

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Conduit is a free, open-source, single-binary Matrix homeserver written in Rust for self-hosting your own federated chat server.

enrichment.short_description: Community-driven, actively maintained Matrix homeserver in Rust, continuing the Conduwuit/Conduit lineage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Continuwuity is a Matrix homeserver written in Rust, positioned as the official community-driven continuation of the conduwuit project, which was itself a hard fork of the original Conduit homeserver. It was started in 2025 after conduwuit was abandoned by its maintainers, to keep that codebase alive with ongoing releases.

The project is open source and community-maintained, hosted across Forgejo, Codeberg, and GitHub mirrors, with a stated goal of a new release roughly every one to two weeks. Existing conduwuit users can migrate by simply changing the container image name in their deployment, since Continuwuity aims to stay a drop-in replacement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Continuwuity is aimed at self-hosters running (or considering) a Matrix homeserver who want an actively maintained option, including existing conduwuit users whose original project stopped receiving updates.

features.title: Matrix protocol federation
features.description: Functions as a full Matrix homeserver, federating with the wider Matrix network.

features.title: Drop-in conduwuit migration
features.description: Compatible with existing conduwuit deployments — migration is typically just a container image change.

features.title: Rust implementation
features.description: Built in Rust for performance and memory safety.

features.title: Regular release cadence
features.description: New releases roughly every one to two weeks from an active community.

features.title: Multiple deployment methods
features.description: Supports Docker and NixOS deployment, among other methods.

features.title: Bridge support
features.description: Works with Matrix bridges to platforms like Discord and Telegram.

pros: Actively maintained with a frequent release cadence, unlike the now-abandoned conduwuit
Free, open source, and self-hostable
Drop-in compatible with existing conduwuit deployments
Community-governed rather than dependent on a single maintainer
cons: Very young project (started 2025), with a shorter track record than established homeservers like Synapse
As a community fork of a fork, long-term project continuity depends on volunteer contributors
Licensing is mixed across sub-repositories rather than a single unified license

faqs.question: Is Continuwuity a fork of Conduit?
faqs.answer: It's the community continuation of conduwuit, which was itself a hard fork of the original Conduit Matrix homeserver.

faqs.question: Why was Continuwuity created?
faqs.answer: conduwuit was abandoned by its maintainers in 2025; Continuwuity was started by community contributors to keep the codebase actively developed.

faqs.question: Can I migrate from conduwuit to Continuwuity?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's designed as a drop-in replacement — migration is generally just changing the container image name.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted Matrix homeserver
use_cases.description: Run your own federated Matrix chat server with active upstream maintenance.

use_cases.title: conduwuit migration
use_cases.description: Move an existing conduwuit deployment to an actively maintained continuation.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Continuwuity is a free, open-source, actively maintained Matrix homeserver in Rust that continues the conduwuit/Conduit project.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source software framework for running a secure, anonymous whistleblowing submission platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GlobaLeaks is free, open-source software, licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3, that lets organizations run their own secure and anonymous whistleblowing platform. The software project began in 2010–2011 and is developed by the Hermes Center for Transparency and Digital Human Rights, an Italian digital-rights NGO.

It's built as a customizable framework rather than a single fixed product: organizations self-host their own instance and configure intake forms, workflows, and anonymity protections for reporters. GlobaLeaks supports over 70 languages and is designed with compliance considerations for standards such as the EU Whistleblowing Directive (2019/1937), ISO 37002, and GDPR.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: GlobaLeaks is used by media organizations, activist and civil-society groups, public agencies, and corporations that need to operate a secure channel for anonymous reports or whistleblower disclosures, particularly where legal whistleblower-protection compliance is required.

features.title: Anonymous submission channel
features.description: Lets whistleblowers submit reports without revealing their identity.

features.title: Customizable intake framework
features.description: Organizations configure their own forms, workflows, and questionnaires.

features.title: Multilingual support
features.description: Available in over 70 languages.

features.title: Regulatory-oriented design
features.description: Built with EU Whistleblowing Directive, ISO 37002, and GDPR considerations in mind.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Organizations run their own instance rather than relying on a third-party host.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hostable under the AGPLv3 license
Purpose-built for anonymity and whistleblower protection, not a repurposed general form tool
Widely translated (70+ languages)
Long track record — actively developed since 2011
cons: Requires self-hosting and ongoing security/administration responsibility, which matters for a tool handling sensitive disclosures
As a compliance-adjacent tool, correct configuration for legal requirements in your jurisdiction is the operator's responsibility
Community/NGO-backed project rather than a commercial vendor with formal SLAs

faqs.question: Is GlobaLeaks free?
faqs.answer: Yes, GlobaLeaks is free, open-source software under the AGPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Who maintains GlobaLeaks?
faqs.answer: It's developed by the Hermes Center for Transparency and Digital Human Rights, an Italian NGO.

faqs.question: Does GlobaLeaks help with whistleblower law compliance?
faqs.answer: It's designed with frameworks such as the EU Whistleblowing Directive, ISO 37002, and GDPR in mind, though correct configuration for a specific jurisdiction is up to the operator.

use_cases.title: Corporate whistleblower channel
use_cases.description: Run an internal reporting channel to meet whistleblower-protection regulations.

use_cases.title: Investigative journalism tip line
use_cases.description: Offer sources a secure, anonymous way to submit leaks or documents.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: GlobaLeaks is free, open-source software for running a secure, anonymous whistleblowing platform, developed by the Hermes Center since 2011.

enrichment.short_description: GNU project's peer-to-peer networking framework for building secure, decentralized, privacy-preserving applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GNUnet is a GNU project providing a network protocol stack for building secure, decentralized, and privacy-preserving applications, licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 (or later). It started in 2001, originally as anonymous, censorship-resistant file sharing, and has since grown into a broader set of building blocks for secure peer-to-peer networking, including resource discovery, naming, and encrypted communication channels.

As a decentralized, self-organizing network, GNUnet has no reliance on central servers. It's primarily a developer framework/protocol stack rather than a polished end-user application — applications are built on top of it rather than GNUnet itself being a single consumer product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: GNUnet is aimed at developers and researchers building privacy-preserving, decentralized applications or protocols, and at technically advanced users interested in running peer-to-peer network infrastructure that protects metadata as well as content.

features.title: Decentralized peer-to-peer architecture
features.description: Self-organizing network with no central point of control.

features.title: Metadata-preserving routing
features.description: Designed to protect sender/receiver identity and communication patterns, not just message content.

features.title: Encrypted communication channels
features.description: Provides building blocks for secure, encrypted networking between peers.

features.title: Naming and resource discovery services
features.description: Includes decentralized naming and addressing components for applications built on top of it.

features.title: Free software
features.description: Released under the GNU AGPLv3+ license as part of the GNU Project.

pros: Free and open source under the GNU AGPLv3+ license
Long-running research and development history (since 2001) backed by academic contributors
Designed around metadata privacy, not just content encryption
Fully decentralized with no dependency on central servers
cons: It's a protocol/framework for developers, not a ready-to-use end-user chat or file-sharing app
Smaller ecosystem and user base than mainstream P2P or messaging networks
Requires technical expertise to deploy and build applications on top of it

faqs.question: Is GNUnet a chat app?
faqs.answer: No — it's a decentralized networking framework/protocol stack that applications (including messaging tools) can be built on top of.

faqs.question: Is GNUnet free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free software released under the GNU AGPLv3+ license.

faqs.question: Who develops GNUnet?
faqs.answer: It's a GNU project, with development led by Christian Grothoff and contributors since 2001.

use_cases.title: Privacy-preserving application development
use_cases.description: Build decentralized applications on top of GNUnet's networking, naming, and encryption primitives.

use_cases.title: Censorship-resistant infrastructure
use_cases.description: Use GNUnet's decentralized routing to avoid single points of control or failure.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: GNUnet is a free, open-source GNU project networking framework for building secure, decentralized, privacy-preserving peer-to-peer applications.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, decentralized peer-to-peer network for censorship-resistant, anonymous publishing and communication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hyphanet is the continuation of the original Freenet project, which began as Ian Clarke's student project at the University of Edinburgh in 1999 and grew into one of the earliest anonymous, censorship-resistant peer-to-peer publishing networks. In 2023, the original Freenet codebase was spun off under the name Hyphanet, maintained by its existing team, while the Freenet name and organization moved to a separate, newer project.

Hyphanet is a fully decentralized network with no central servers: users can connect to the public Opennet, build trusted friend-to-friend (darknet) links, or bridge offline via sneakernets. A plugin system enables use cases like personal websites (freesites), forums, microblogging, and file sharing on top of the anonymous network layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Hyphanet suits people who need censorship-resistant publishing or communication — for example under surveillance or content-blocking conditions — and who are willing to trade convenience and speed for a decentralized, anonymous network with no central point of failure.

features.title: Decentralized, serverless network
features.description: No central authority or server required to publish or retrieve content.

features.title: Opennet and darknet modes
features.description: Connect to the public network or build friend-to-friend trusted links.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Plugins add personal websites, forums, microblogging, and file/media sharing.

features.title: Sneakernet bridging
features.description: Supports offline data exchange to bridge isolated network segments.

features.title: Anonymity by design
features.description: Built around anonymous publishing and communication rather than added as an afterthought.

pros: Free and open source with a very long operating history (since 1999)
Fully decentralized — no central servers to seize, block, or shut down
Supports both public (Opennet) and trusted friend-to-friend (darknet) modes
Actively maintained, with builds released through 2025–2026
cons: Network performance and content availability depend on peer participation, and can be slower than centralized alternatives
The 2023 Freenet/Hyphanet rebrand can cause confusion about which project ('Freenet' or 'Hyphanet') a given resource refers to
Steeper learning curve than mainstream messaging or publishing tools

faqs.question: Is Hyphanet the same as Freenet?
faqs.answer: Hyphanet is the continuation of the original Freenet codebase (started in 1999), rebranded in 2023 after the Freenet name and organization moved to a separate, newer project.

faqs.question: Is Hyphanet free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free, open-source software.

faqs.question: Does Hyphanet require central servers?
faqs.answer: No, it's a fully decentralized peer-to-peer network.

use_cases.title: Censorship-resistant publishing
use_cases.description: Publish freesites, forums, or files that remain accessible even if individual peers go offline.

use_cases.title: Anonymous communication under surveillance
use_cases.description: Communicate and share information without relying on centralized, blockable infrastructure.
use_cases.audience: Users in high-censorship or high-surveillance environments

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hyphanet (formerly Freenet, since 1999) is a free, open-source, decentralized peer-to-peer network for censorship-resistant anonymous publishing.
